findings.title: Weak Content-Security-Policy Configuration
findings.observation: The Content-Security-Policy contains unsafe-inline or wildcard directives, which weakens script source guarantees.
findings.evidence: CSP: default-src blob: 'self' https://*.fbsbx.com *.facebook.com *.fbcdn.net *.facebook.net *.whatsapp.com *.whatsapp.net;script-src *.facebook.com *.fbcdn.net *.facebook.net findings.aiAnalysis: The CSP contains directives like unsafe-inline or wildcard hosts. While a policy is present, these directives bypass some of the primary XSS mitigations.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: Unsafe-inline or wildcard values are sometimes required for legacy script compatibility or CDN hosting. This is reported as a hardening recommendation. [Validation Review: Exploitability not demonstrated. Downgraded to Best Practice.]
findings.businessImpact: Increases the risk of XSS execution if a parameter reflection vulnerability is introduced.
findings.remediation: Refactor client-side code to use event listeners instead of inline scripts, and remove unsafe-inline and wildcard source hosts from CSP.

findings.description: The Content-Security-Policy contains unsafe-inline or wildcard directives, which weakens script source guarantees.
findings.reasoning: The CSP contains directives like unsafe-inline or wildcard hosts. While a policy is present, these directives bypass some of the primary XSS mitigations.

findings.title: Strict-Transport-Security Preloaded (Mitigated)
findings.observation: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) header is missing on the raw response, but the domain is preloaded in browsers.
findings.evidence: Domain is in HSTS Preload registry
findings.detectionLogic: Match hostname against HSTS Preload registry
findings.aiAnalysis: No HSTS header was observed. However, the target is a known enterprise domain preloaded in modern browsers via HSTS Preload lists. Therefore, the lack of an explicit HSTS header on the response does not present an exploitable risk.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: HSTS preload lists are built directly into modern browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari), which force HTTPS immediately before any connection. This fully mitigates the risk. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: None. Browser security enforcement prevents any plain-text transmission to this domain.
findings.remediation: No remediation required. The domain is fully secured via the HSTS Preload registry.

findings.description: The Referrer-Policy header is missing, which could leak sensitive parameters or tokens in referrer headers to external hosts.
findings.reasoning: No Referrer-Policy header was observed. The browser's default behavior applies, which might send URL paths including query strings to third parties.

findings.title: HTTP Method Ignored: PUT
findings.observation: Testing the PUT method returned status 200, but the response behaves identically to a GET request. The method is likely ignored by the routing engine.
findings.evidence: Method: PUT | Status: 200 | GET Similarity: True (Length diff: 0 bytes)
findings.detectionLogic: Compare HTTP method response status and body content with baseline GET response
findings.aiAnalysis: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: The server did not execute any state-changing operations and returned default GET output. No actual vulnerability exists. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: None. No state modifications can be performed through this method.
findings.remediation: No remediation needed. The server ignores the method or routes it to the GET handler.

findings.aiAnalysis: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: The server did not execute any state-changing operations and returned default GET output. No actual vulnerability exists. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: None. No state modifications can be performed through this method.
findings.remediation: No remediation needed. The server ignores the method or routes it to the GET handler.

findings.description: Testing the PATCH method returned status 200, but the response behaves identically to a GET request. The method is likely ignored by the routing engine.
findings.reasoning: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.

findings.title: HSTS Max-Age Too Low
findings.observation: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) max-age is set to less than 1 year (31,536,000 seconds).
findings.detectionLogic: Parse max-age parameter from strict-transport-security header and check value
findings.aiAnalysis: The HSTS header has a max-age shorter than 31,536,000 seconds. While transport security is enforced, the duration is shorter than the industry-recommended standard.
findings.falsePositiveAssessment: A low max-age is often used during testing or migrations to prevent long-term lockouts. It should be increased once HTTPS stability is verified. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
findings.businessImpact: Transport security guarantees will expire sooner than expected.
findings.remediation: Increase the Strict-Transport-Security max-age directive to at least 31536000.

allFindings.title: Weak Content-Security-Policy Configuration
allFindings.observation: The Content-Security-Policy contains unsafe-inline or wildcard directives, which weakens script source guarantees.

allFindings.aiAnalysis: The CSP contains directives like unsafe-inline or wildcard hosts. While a policy is present, these directives bypass some of the primary XSS mitigations.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: Unsafe-inline or wildcard values are sometimes required for legacy script compatibility or CDN hosting. This is reported as a hardening recommendation. [Validation Review: Exploitability not demonstrated. Downgraded to Best Practice.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Increases the risk of XSS execution if a parameter reflection vulnerability is introduced.
allFindings.remediation: Refactor client-side code to use event listeners instead of inline scripts, and remove unsafe-inline and wildcard source hosts from CSP.

allFindings.description: The Referrer-Policy header is missing, which could leak sensitive parameters or tokens in referrer headers to external hosts.
allFindings.reasoning: No Referrer-Policy header was observed. The browser's default behavior applies, which might send URL paths including query strings to third parties.

allFindings.title: HTTP Method Ignored: PUT
allFindings.observation: Testing the PUT method returned status 200, but the response behaves identically to a GET request. The method is likely ignored by the routing engine.
allFindings.evidence: Method: PUT | Status: 200 | GET Similarity: True (Length diff: 0 bytes)
allFindings.detectionLogic: Compare HTTP method response status and body content with baseline GET response
allFindings.aiAnalysis: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: The server did not execute any state-changing operations and returned default GET output. No actual vulnerability exists. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: None. No state modifications can be performed through this method.
allFindings.remediation: No remediation needed. The server ignores the method or routes it to the GET handler.

allFindings.aiAnalysis: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: The server did not execute any state-changing operations and returned default GET output. No actual vulnerability exists. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: None. No state modifications can be performed through this method.
allFindings.remediation: No remediation needed. The server ignores the method or routes it to the GET handler.

allFindings.description: Testing the PATCH method returned status 200, but the response behaves identically to a GET request. The method is likely ignored by the routing engine.
allFindings.reasoning: The server accepted the method request, but returned the exact homepage/GET response. It is highly likely the router ignores the method and treats it as a GET.

allFindings.title: HSTS Max-Age Too Low
allFindings.observation: The Strict-Transport-Security (HSTS) max-age is set to less than 1 year (31,536,000 seconds).
allFindings.detectionLogic: Parse max-age parameter from strict-transport-security header and check value
allFindings.aiAnalysis: The HSTS header has a max-age shorter than 31,536,000 seconds. While transport security is enforced, the duration is shorter than the industry-recommended standard.
allFindings.falsePositiveAssessment: A low max-age is often used during testing or migrations to prevent long-term lockouts. It should be increased once HTTPS stability is verified. [Validation Review: Downgraded to Informational due to negligible exploit path proof.]
allFindings.businessImpact: Transport security guarantees will expire sooner than expected.
allFindings.remediation: Increase the Strict-Transport-Security max-age directive to at least 31536000.
